---
title: "Finding Local Stories in ICE Arrest and Detainer Data"
subtitle: "A Workshop on the Deportation Data Project"
author: "Raphael Romero Ruiz"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    footer: "deportationdata.org • rromeror@umd.edu"
    code-fold: false
    incremental: false
execute:
  echo: false
---

## Before We Start {.center}

**Pre-workshop survey:** What coverage area are you reporting on, and what questions do you most want to answer?

[Survey link — add your Google Form URL here]

Also: Is your data downloaded? Run `setup.R` if you haven't already.

---

## The Paper Trail {.center}

Millions of records.
One enforcement surge.
Your story is in here.

---

## Where Does This Data Come From?

**The Deportation Data Project** publishes internal U.S. government immigration enforcement data obtained through Freedom of Information Act litigation.

The March 2026 release includes:

- ICE arrests — **540,000+ records** across FY2023–FY2026
- ICE detainers — who was asked to hold someone, and whether they complied
- ICE detentions — where people were held and for how long
- ICE encounters — pre-arrest contact events

All obtained through a FOIA lawsuit — the same legal mechanism you use for local records.

**Data source:** [deportationdata.org](https://deportationdata.org)

---

## Three Datasets — What Each Captures

| Dataset | What it records | Key question |
|---------|----------------|-------------|
| **Arrests** | Every ICE arrest — who, where, how | How many arrests in my area? How? |
| **Detainers** | ICE requests to local jails | Is my sheriff cooperating? |
| **Detentions** | Where detainees are held | Where do people from my area end up? |

Today we focus on **arrests and detainers** — the most actionable for local stories.

---

## The Enforcement Pipeline

```
Local jail holds someone
        ↓
ICE issues a detainer (request to hold)
        ↓
Jail says yes → ICE picks them up [Custodial Arrest]
        ↓
ICE processing facility (Baltimore, Salisbury...)
        ↓
Detention facility (often out of state)
        ↓
Immigration court → Deportation or Release
```

The **detainers file** lives at the second step.
The **arrests file** covers both the custodial path and at-large community arrests.

---

## What the Data Does NOT Cover

- **No CBP** — Customs and Border Protection (border crossings) is a separate agency with separate data
- **No names** — all person identifiers are anonymized
- **No real-time data** — the current release runs through March 10, 2026
- **Some redacted fields** — certain facility details and case-level information are withheld

This is still the most detailed public record ever produced on ICE interior enforcement.

---

## Custodial vs. Non-Custodial

Two types of arrest — both in the same data file.

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Custodial**
ICE gets a call from a jail.
Picks up someone already in custody.
*Examples: 287(g) program, CAP*
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Non-Custodial (At-Large)**
ICE finds someone in the community.
No prior custody.
*Examples: home raids, worksite enforcement, fugitive operations*
:::

:::

In the FY24–FY26 data: **~285,000 custodial** / **~250,000 non-custodial**

---

## The July 2025 Change

**Before July 2025:** ICE reported CAP program arrests separately — you could distinguish `CAP Local Incarceration`, `CAP Federal Incarceration`, and `287(g) Program`.

**After July 2025:** ICE folded all CAP arrests into a single `"Custodial Arrest"` category.

**What this means for you:**

- ✅ Comparing custodial vs. non-custodial totals is fine across the full range
- ⚠️ Comparing 287(g) vs. CAP specifically only works before July 2025

This is your first **nerd box** moment — a methodology note your readers need.

---

## Stories Built From This Data

**Capital News Service (Nov. 2025)**
"Maryland's surprising role in implementing Trump's immigration agenda"
→ 2,000+ arrests. 70% had no prior conviction.

**Capital News Service (Dec. 2025)**
"MD immigrants detained by ICE find themselves far from home"
→ 3 in 4 Maryland detainees sent to Louisiana or Texas.

**Milwaukee Journal Sentinel (Apr. 2026)**
"ICE arrests of people without convictions grows sixfold in Wisconsin"
→ The criminality breakdown, before and after inauguration.

**New York Focus (Apr. 2026)**
"Local Police in Westchester, Long Island Routinely Help ICE Make Arrests"
→ Detainer compliance rate by agency.

---

## What You'll Build Today

By the end of this workshop, you'll have:

- Loaded and cleaned the full FY24–FY26 arrests and detainers data
- Filtered it to your coverage area
- Produced a monthly trend chart with the inauguration marker
- Found your local detainer compliance rate
- Identified one story angle with a publishable number

And you'll take home the `my_newsroom/` template — change two lines of code, re-run, get your local analysis.

---

## Three Lessons, 50 Minutes Each

| | Lesson | Key skill |
|--|--------|----------|
| 📂 | **Lesson 1: Loading & Cleaning** | `read_xlsx()`, `filter()`, `count()` |
| 📊 | **Lesson 2: Summarizing & Joining** | `group_by()`, `summarize()`, `left_join()` |
| 📈 | **Lesson 3: Visualization & Stories** | `ggplot2`, story hunting |

10-minute break between Lessons 2 and 3.

---

## Before We Open RStudio

Check:

- [ ] R and RStudio installed
- [ ] `ddp-workshop.Rproj` open (look for the project name in the upper-right corner)
- [ ] FOIA files downloaded to `data/raw/`
- [ ] `setup.R` run once

If you're missing the data files, download them now:
**deportationdata.org/data/ice.html** → 2026-ICLI-00005 release → Arrests FY24, FY25, FY26 + Detainers FY24, FY25, FY26

Any questions before we start?
